content_markdown: An ideal gas undergoes adiabatic expansion from state X to a new state of volume $V$. During this process the work done by the gas is $W$. What is the change in internal energy and the work done in an **isothermal** expansion of this gas from X to $V$?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A-D) with columns for 'Change in internal energy' and 'Work done'.

options.label: A
options.text: Change in internal energy: 0; Work done: greater than $W$

options.label: B
options.text: Change in internal energy: 0; Work done: less than $W$

options.label: C
options.text: Change in internal energy: greater than 0; Work done: less than $W$

options.label: D
options.text: Change in internal energy: less than 0; Work done: same as $W$

vibe_explanation: In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the temperature remains constant, so the change in internal energy is 0. On a P-V diagram, an isotherm lies above an adiabat starting from the same point X. Therefore, the area under the curve (work done) for the isothermal process to the same final volume $V$ is greater than the area under the adiabatic curve $W$.
